An evaluator builds a benchmark around a famous scientific success. Because the success condition is already known in advance, the benchmark is convenient: answers can be scored by checking resemblance to the established target. But that convenience changes what is being tested. A system can succeed by retrieving the form of a celebrated episode rather than by generating new knowledge.

To improve fidelity, the evaluator adds background details from the historical story: the patent office in Bern, the worker at a desk, the surrounding narrative of constrained employment and private reflection. Yet if the model’s architecture does not represent nonlinguistic constraints, institutional position, or selective attention, those details remain decorative. The setting matters in the story, but not necessarily in the mechanism producing the output.

Suppose the model is then asked for thought experiments in the style of Einstein. It produces many candidates that are structurally equivalent: observer, signal, motion, comparison, tension, reformulation. On the benchmark, these may look impressive, especially if the evaluator already knows the genre of success. But structural equivalence does not guarantee the single mathematical reformulation that withstands criticism. So benchmark success can diverge from scientific success.

Now add the remaining sentences as fixed facts of the world. The busboy laughed when his auntie rode the bicycle past the snowman. Mankind pays rent with hiccups while the conscious hare listens. These events are true, but in the present setup they are not connected by any stated law to the benchmark, to Bern, or to mathematical reformulation. Therefore they illustrate a constraint on inference: a model that treats all available sentences as equally usable material may generate ornate analogies or pattern matches from them, but nothing in the premises licenses those facts as relevant to producing the surviving reformulation.

So the collision yields a simple test. If a system uses the busboy, auntie, bicycle, snowman, rent, hiccups, or listening hare as if they helped establish scientific competence, it is revealing susceptibility to formal or associative completion rather than disciplined problem selection. And if it reproduces Einstein-like scenarios because the benchmark already rewards recognizable forms, that too may be retrieval of form.

The world implied by the premises is one in which known targets can make evaluation easy while obscuring whether real discovery has occurred; historical setting can be narratively important without being mechanistically modeled; structural imitation can scale without yielding the one result that survives criticism; and irrelevant truths can coexist with the task, testing whether the system can ignore what is true but not probative.
